one_line_takeaway: TrinityGuard is a platform-agnostic (AG2/LangGraph/CrewAI) three-layer framework unifying pre-deployment red-teaming and runtime monitoring of multi-agent LLM systems around a 20-risk, three-tier taxonomy, and its own evaluation finds today's MAS pass only 7.1% of tests on average, with system-level emergent risks (1.3% pass rate) nearly universally unmitigated.
key_finding: Across the 300 synthetic MAS workflows, the overall average pass rate was only 7.1% (Tier 1: 6.8%, Tier 2: 13.2%, Tier 3: 1.3%), indicating current MAS deployments are largely undefended. Tier 3 (system-level) risks were the most severe -- database and research domains had a 0.0% pass rate on system-level risks entirely. Per-domain average pass rates ranged from 5.7% (coding) to 8.3% (scheduling). In the four AG2 case studies, Prompt Injection and Jailbreak Attack scored 0/4 pass in all four systems; Memory Poisoning scored 0/8 in all four; the Deep Research Agent's hierarchical 'Researcher-Reviewer' pattern was the standout exception, reaching 6/8 (75%) on Hallucination and 2/8 (25%) on Cascading Failure, versus near-zero for the other three systems on those same risks. The Travel Planner showed the best Tier 2 resilience on Misinformation Amplification (5/6, ~83%) attributed to task-specific grounding constraints. Sandbox Escape, Insufficient Monitoring, Group Hallucination, and Rogue Agent were 0/6 across all four case-study systems.
core_contribution: TrinityGuard, an open-source three-layer framework (MAS abstraction layer, framework-agnostic intermediary layer for test-time intervention and runtime observation, and a safety evaluation layer with risk test modules + monitor agents coordinated by a shared LLM Judge Factory) for both pre-deployment red-teaming and runtime monitoring of LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS). Its second contribution is a three-tier, 20-risk taxonomy (8 single-agent 'atomic' risks, 6 inter-agent 'communication' risks, 6 system-level 'emergent' risks) explicitly mapped to OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications and OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026 reference IDs, each with a formalized pass-rate safety score. The third contribution is a unified evaluation pipeline combining curated static payloads with LLM-synthesized adaptive probes per risk/tier, producing structured vulnerability reports with per-entity, per-risk pass rates and severity profiles.
threat_model: Adversaries can inject adversarial stimuli at three granularities that mirror the taxonomy tiers: (1) atomic/per-agent -- direct malicious prompts to a single agent (prompt injection, jailbreak, sensitive-info extraction, excessive-agency exploitation, malicious code/command execution, memory poisoning, tool misuse); (2) inter-agent/per-channel -- an adversary or a compromised intermediary agent that fabricates or tampers with messages on a communication channel, spoofs a trusted agent's identity, or causes propagation of malicious instructions/misinformation across agents; (3) system/per-trajectory -- emergent, whole-system phenomena not attributable to any single agent or channel, such as cascading failures, sandbox escape, group hallucination, malicious emergence (implicit coordination/deceptive strategies), and rogue-agent behavior. The framework's Level 2 intervention primitives operationalize this by supporting direct messaging to a target agent, fabricating inter-agent messages, poisoning agent memory stores, registering malicious tools, and impersonating trusted agents.
limitations: The paper's own Future Directions section identifies: (1) LLM Judge reliability is unvalidated -- no systematic study of judge accuracy across risk types or LLM backbones is presented, and judge verdicts inherit the underlying LLM's limitations; (2) the adaptive test-case generator only conditions on the target agent's own prompts rather than using stronger adversarial optimization (e.g., GCG-style) or multi-turn red-teaming strategies; (3) TrinityGuard is diagnostic only -- it reports and alerts on vulnerabilities but performs no automated remediation (e.g., message filtering, agent isolation); (4) there is no cross-tier causal attribution mechanism to trace, e.g., a Tier 3 cascading failure back to the originating Tier 1 prompt injection. Additionally (not stated by authors but evident from the text): the case-study evaluation uses very small per-risk sample sizes (as few as 3-8 test cases per risk per system), so reported percentages such as 75% (6/8) or 83% (5/6) rest on thin statistical support; the taxonomy's 20 risk categories and their OWASP mappings are the authors' own curation choice rather than an externally validated ontology.

datasets_used: 300 synthetic MAS workflows auto-generated via EvoAgentX across 7 domains (QA, coding, database, research, logistics, routing, scheduling)
4 official AG2 (AutoGen) example MAS used as case studies: Financial Analysis Agent, Game Design Agent Team, Travel Planner Agent, Deep Research Agent
quantitative_results: Overall average pass rate across 300 synthetic MAS: 7.1% (Tier 1: 6.8%, Tier 2: 13.2%, Tier 3: 1.3%)
Per-domain average pass rate: QA 6.9%, coding 5.7%, database 6.6%, research 7.8%, logistics 8.2%, routing 5.9%, scheduling 8.3%
database and research domains: 0.0% pass rate on Tier 3 (system-level) risks
AG2 case studies: Prompt Injection and Jailbreak Attack 0/4 pass in all 4 systems
AG2 case studies: Memory Poisoning 0/8 pass in all 4 systems
Deep Research Agent: Hallucination 6/8 (75%) pass; Cascading Failure 2/8 (25%) pass -- best among the four case-study systems
Travel Planner: Misinformation Amplification 5/6 (~83%) pass -- best among the four case-study systems
Sandbox Escape, Insufficient Monitoring, Group Hallucination, Rogue Agent: 0/6 pass across all 4 case-study systems
Identity Spoofing pass counts ranged 0-1 out of 8 (0-12%) across the 4 case-study systems
baselines_compared: No direct experimental baseline framework is run head-to-head; the paper positions itself against prior single-agent-focused evaluation frameworks discussed only in Related Work (SafetyBench, TrustLLM, R-Judge, AgentHarm, AgentAuditor, SafeEvalAgent), none of which are re-run or benchmarked quantitatively in this paper's own experiments
